notes.anchor: the eight-year shadow of the Junian trial
notes.note: The Junian trial of 74 BC, in which Cluentius prosecuted his stepfather Oppianicus for attempted poisoning and the presiding judge Iunius was later himself prosecuted for accepting bribes from Cluentius. The Junian verdict's reputation hangs over the entire defence in Pro Cluentio.
Sassia of Larinum, Cluentius's mother and (Cicero alleges) the prime mover behind the prosecution; one of the great hostile portraits in Latin literature.

notes.anchor: in public meetings the place of hatred, in trials of truth
notes.note: Cicero's closing line: contiones loci sint odii, iudicia sint loci veritatis. A formula often quoted for the late-Republican optimate distinction between forum populare (the contio) and forum forense (the court).
